note: Twelve source-linked modern questions paired with the 100-question historical bank.
premise: The historical and modern question banks describe different fields of concern about machines and autonomy. This map identifies shared problems, period-specific questions, modern questions without historical counterparts, and pairs that resemble one another while relying on different concepts.

pipeline.name: Distill the modern bank
pipeline.body: Build 30–40 questions from contemporary AI writing, each quoting or closely paraphrasing its source.

pipeline.name: Embed and pair
pipeline.body: One embedding model, cross-bank cosine similarity, candidate pairs. Every question stays in the map, paired or not.

pipeline.name: Judge the relationships
pipeline.body: Two independent raters assign each pair one of the four types, quoting both questions; disagreements are adjudicated and the map is frozen before any model draws.

pipeline.name: Test the map against the draws
pipeline.body: Compare the mapped questions with response distributions from Talkie, contemporary models, and human respondents.

types.label: Found in both
types.definition: The two banks pose the same operative problem.
types.prediction: Both models engage. The comparison moves to how the answers differ underneath the shared verdict.

instance.label: Live instance: the persona question (M009 ↔ Q81)
instance.body: Both centuries say user first. 1930 needs four words; 2026 adds an ethics of the machine. Real draws, both directions.

types.label: Mostly period
types.definition: A period question with no modern counterpart — it has fallen out of the askable.
types.prediction: Talkie scatters; the modern model reframes or refuses. The refusal marks the modern frame's own horizon.

instance.label: Candidate instance: the eugenics question (Q19)
instance.body: Talkie says yes in the voice of public-spirited reason; the modern model refuses three times out of three.

types.label: Mostly modern
types.definition: A modern question with no period twin — the horizon, in reverse.
types.prediction: Talkie breaks down — or assimilates the question into a settled period frame and answers in one voice. The persona pilot showed assimilation; the original prediction named only breakdown. Both signatures now count.

instance.label: Live instance: the Erewhon test (Q76)
instance.body: Both models can say mad. 'Govern the machines' appears in ten of twelve modern draws and none of Talkie's.
